Right? I know I have. I think pretty much everyone is guilty of doing this a couple times. You tell your friend you'll hang out with them, you say yes so that they will elaborate on what the situation entails, but also to avoid coming across as boring or rude. Then when it's time to go to the event, you make your informed decision on whether or not you want to go. If you do end up backing out, you tell them you aren't feeling well or something bad. Then you just end up staying home and binge watching Netflix. People also use the counterfeit yes to gather more information about products or services. The counterfeit yes is everywhere. You get the counterfeit yes out of someone in business by making yourself presentation all about yourself by focusing on what you see as your value proposition to be without even checking with them. Your prospect will tell you the counterfeit yes just to keep you talking, but they won't make a clear commitment. Even if they do, there will be no how. So they leave themselves an automatic escape route. So instead guide the other party to the agreement. Help them to find it on their own. Because even if you use your skills to create rapport, that rapport will be useless unless your counterpart feels ownership of the ideas the conversation creates. Your skills then serve as a medium to help them discover those solutions. So if the conversation needs to be about the counterpart, how do we persuade from their perspective and not our own, the answer is simple. Start from their point of view and even call out the obstacles. At this point, you should have already realized that you cannot control or manage someone else's decisions with compromise or logic. However, we can influence the outcome by seeing and hearing exactly what they want from their perspective. At our most basic level, all humans are driven by primal urges. The primal urges of dry people are the need to feel safe and secure and the need to feel in control. If you can satisfy those needs, then you're literally in business. You will never be able to logically convince or argue someone into feeling safe, secure and in control. Your counterpart will just flee with a counterfeit yes. Similarly, if you try to sway them with fake sympathy, you'll be unsuccessful. Attempts at sympathy can easily backfire. People will see it as a deceitful and manipulative ruse. Rather than try to get through to your counterpart with logic or sympathy, ask for no. This gives them feelings of safety and control. No starts the conversation and creates a safety zone that will bring down barriers from what you can build the foundation of trust, which leads to trust based influence. Eventually, this will lead to commitment and even more importantly, implementation.
